1 import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'; 2 import { getCachedProducts } from '@/lib/products'; 3 4 /** 5 * Simulates a long-running catalog sync job (e.g. fetching updated pricing, 6 * inventory, and availability from an upstream ERP/PIM system). 7 * 8 * In production this would enqueue a background worker, call an external API, 9 * or write to a job table. The key design property is that the *caller* does 10 * not await this promise — the page responds immediately and the cache is 11 * invalidated only after the sync finishes. 12 */ 13 async function syncJob(): Promise { 14 console.log('[Catalog Sync] background sync started'); 15 16 // Simulate upstream data fetch — thousands of SKUs 17 // In production: fetchDeltaFromERP(), writeToStaging(), etc. 18 await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(() => resolve(), 5_000)); 19 20 console.log('[Catalog Sync] sync complete, revalidating cache tag "products"'); 21 22 // Invalidate every view that depends on the "products" cache tag. 23 // The next request to any such view will re-execute its data function 24 // and serve fresh data. 25 revalidateTag('products', {}); 26 } 27 28 export default async function AdminCatalogPage() { 29 const products = await getCachedProducts(); 30 31 /** 32 * Inline Server Action — no client JavaScript required for the form submit. 33 * 34 * Fires the background sync and returns immediately so the admin can 35 * continue working. The product list may show stale data for a few 36 * seconds until `syncJob` finishes and invalidates the `"products"` tag. 37 */ 38 async function syncCatalog(): Promise { 39 'use server'; 40 41 console.log('[Catalog Sync] trigger received'); 42 43 // Fire-and-forget: the action returns immediately; the cache is 44 // invalidated only *after* the sync completes. 45 syncJob().catch((err) => { 46 console.error('[Catalog Sync] background job failed', err); 47 }); 48 } 49 50 return ( 51
